title: Jerk seasoning paste
subtitle: Jamaican scotch bonnet and allspice marinade
excerpt: A wet paste of scotch bonnet, allspice, spring onion, thyme, ginger, garlic, and dark rum, the foundational marinade of Jamaican jerk cooking. This is the seasoning that goes on pork, chicken, and fish before grilling or roasting. Made in quantity and kept in the fridge, it is the most useful thing to have on hand for Jamaican cooking. The heat is significant; the allspice gives it its distinctive warm, sweet undertone.
sourceNotes: Jerk seasoning is the defining flavour of Jamaican cooking, a marinade tradition rooted in the Maroon communities of Jamaica who developed the technique of preserving and seasoning meat with allspice (pimento) and scotch bonnet before smoking it over pimento wood. Commercial jerk pastes and dry rubs are widely available, but the home-made wet paste has a freshness and heat that no commercial product matches. Every Jamaican cook has their own proportions and additions.

content.text: The scotch bonnet is not negotiable. Jerk seasoning without scotch bonnet is not jerk seasoning, the fruity heat of the scotch bonnet is what distinguishes it from other chilli-based marinades. Use the full quantity the first time; adjust downward if needed once you know how much heat your scotch bonnets carry. They vary considerably in heat level.

Allspice berries (whole, freshly ground) are the other non-negotiable. Pre-ground allspice works but is weaker, if using, double the quantity. The paste keeps for two weeks in a sealed jar in the fridge and the flavour improves after a day or two as the ingredients meld.

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: deseeded for less heat, or left whole

items.name: Allspice
items.prepNote: whole berries, freshly ground, or 1 tbsp pre-ground

content.text: If using whole {{allspice}} berries, grind them in a spice grinder or with a pestle and mortar. Combine all ingredients in a food processor or blender: {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{allspice}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{nutmeg}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{black-pepper}}, {{browning-sauce}}, {{rum-dark}}, and {{vegetable-oil}}. Blitz to a rough paste. The texture should be coarse, not perfectly smooth, some texture is desirable. Taste and adjust: more scotch bonnet for heat, more allspice for warmth, more salt if flat. Transfer to a sealed jar and refrigerate.

items.fix: Add 1 to 2 tablespoons of water or more vegetable oil and blend again. The paste should be spreadable, not powdery
Reduce to 2 scotch bonnets and add half a red pepper instead for body without the extra heat. Scotch bonnets vary enormously in heat, a single batch can be mild, the next fiercely hot
items.cause: Particularly hot scotch bonnets or too many

items.fix: Pre-ground spices lose potency quickly. Use freshly ground whole berries, or double the quantity of pre-ground. Allspice is the heart of jerk, if it is weak, the paste will be generic
items.cause: Pre-ground allspice too old, or insufficient allspice

content.text: Jerk seasoning is the taste of Jamaica more than any other flavour, the smell of jerk pork or chicken smoking over pimento wood is inseparable from the Jamaican landscape and its diaspora. The paste goes on chicken, pork, fish, and vegetables before grilling, roasting, or smoking. It keeps well and improves with age in the jar. In Britain, jerk chicken is one of the most widely eaten Caribbean dishes, sold at street food markets, from jerk centres, and made at home across the Caribbean diaspora as the essential weekend barbecue seasoning. The home-made paste is considerably more complex than any bottled version.
